tokenGroup.name: color-text
tokenGroup.description: Semantic color tokens for text content. These tokens map to base palette values and adapt across light and dark color modes.

tokens.name: color-text-primary
tokens.description: The default color for body text, headings, and labels.

guidelines.guidance: Use for all body text, headings, and form labels on default background surfaces.
guidelines.rationale: A single primary text color ensures visual consistency and meets WCAG 2.1 AA contrast requirements against the system's default background.

guidelines.guidance: Do not use on dark or colored background surfaces. Use color-text-inverse or color-text-on-action instead.
guidelines.rationale: This token is optimized for contrast against light backgrounds. Using it on dark or saturated surfaces will fail contrast requirements.

guidelines.guidance: Do not override this token's value at the component level. Use color-text-secondary or color-text-tertiary for reduced emphasis.
guidelines.rationale: Overriding the primary text color creates inconsistency. The system provides lower-emphasis text tokens for visual hierarchy.

guidelines.guidance: This color meets a 15.3:1 contrast ratio against color-background-default (#ffffff) in light mode.
guidelines.rationale: Exceeds WCAG 2.1 AAA requirements (7:1 for normal text, 4.5:1 for large text), ensuring readability for users with low vision.

guidelines.guidance: This color meets a 14.8:1 contrast ratio against color-background-default (#121212) in dark mode.
guidelines.rationale: The dark mode value (#e5e5e5) maintains AAA compliance against the dark background.

colorContrast.context: Primary text on default page background in light mode.
Primary text on subtle background (e.g., card surface) in light mode.
Primary text on elevated surface (e.g., modal) in light mode.

tokens.name: color-text-secondary
tokens.description: A reduced-emphasis text color for supporting content such as descriptions, captions, and metadata.

guidelines.guidance: Use for descriptions, helper text, captions, timestamps, and metadata that support a primary heading or label.
guidelines.rationale: Secondary text creates visual hierarchy. It signals to the reader that this content is supplementary, not primary.

guidelines.guidance: Do not use for body text in paragraphs longer than two sentences.
guidelines.rationale: Extended reading at reduced contrast causes eye strain. Body text must use color-text-primary for comfortable reading over sustained periods.

guidelines.guidance: Do not stack secondary and tertiary text without a primary text anchor on the same surface.
guidelines.rationale: A surface with only reduced-emphasis text lacks a clear entry point. Every content group needs at least one primary-emphasis element to anchor the reader's attention.

guidelines.guidance: This color meets a 7.0:1 contrast ratio against color-background-default in light mode. It passes WCAG AA for all text sizes and WCAG AAA for large text (18px bold or 24px regular and above).
guidelines.rationale: Secondary text is commonly used at smaller sizes (captions, metadata), where AA compliance is the minimum acceptable threshold.

colorContrast.context: Secondary text on default page background in light mode.
Secondary text on subtle background (e.g., card surface) in light mode.

tokens.name: color-text-tertiary
tokens.description: The lowest-emphasis text color for placeholder text, disabled labels, and non-critical annotations.

guidelines.guidance: Use for input placeholder text, disabled control labels, and decorative annotations.
guidelines.rationale: Tertiary text is for content the user can ignore without consequence. It is present for context but not required for task completion.

guidelines.guidance: Do not use for error messages, validation text, helper text, or any content the user needs to act on.
guidelines.rationale: Actionable content at tertiary emphasis is easy to miss. Users with low vision may not perceive it at all. Actionable content requires color-text-primary or color-text-secondary at minimum.

guidelines.guidance: Do not use as the only means of communicating a disabled state. Pair with opacity reduction or a disabled icon.
guidelines.rationale: Color alone is insufficient to communicate state changes. Users with color vision deficiency may not distinguish tertiary text from secondary text. A second visual indicator (opacity, icon, strikethrough) ensures the state is perceivable.

guidelines.guidance: This color meets a 3.5:1 contrast ratio against color-background-default in light mode. It passes WCAG AA for large text only (18px bold or 24px regular and above).
guidelines.rationale: Tertiary text is intentionally low-contrast. It is acceptable for placeholder text and disabled labels per WCAG 1.4.3, which exempts inactive UI components and placeholder text from contrast requirements.

guidelines.guidance: Do not use at font sizes below 16px for any content that is not placeholder or disabled.
guidelines.rationale: At small sizes and low contrast, text becomes illegible for users with even moderate vision impairment. Reserve this token for contexts where WCAG provides explicit exemptions.

colorContrast.context: Tertiary text on default page background in light mode. Acceptable for placeholder text and disabled labels only.

tokens.name: color-text-inverse
tokens.description: A high-contrast text color for use on dark or saturated background surfaces.

guidelines.guidance: Use on dark backgrounds (color-background-inverse, color-background-overlay), filled interactive surfaces (primary buttons, selected states), and brand-colored hero sections.
guidelines.rationale: Inverse text is optimized for contrast against dark and saturated surfaces. It maintains readability where color-text-primary would fail.

guidelines.guidance: Do not use on light background surfaces.
guidelines.rationale: White text on a white or near-white background is invisible. This token is not intended for default surface usage.

guidelines.guidance: When placing inverse text on a brand-colored or non-neutral background, verify contrast manually. The system guarantees contrast against neutral dark backgrounds only.
guidelines.rationale: Brand colors and accent surfaces vary in lightness. The system cannot pre-validate every possible combination. Manual contrast verification ensures accessibility is maintained.

guidelines.guidance: This color meets a 16.4:1 contrast ratio against color-background-inverse (#1a1a1a).
guidelines.rationale: Exceeds WCAG 2.1 AAA requirements for all text sizes on the system's standard dark background.

colorContrast.context: Inverse text on the standard dark background.
Inverse text on a primary action surface (e.g., filled button).
Inverse text on a scrim/overlay surface.

tokens.name: color-text-link
tokens.description: The color for inline text links in body content.

guidelines.guidance: Use for inline text links within body content. Always pair with an underline on the text decoration.
guidelines.rationale: Color alone is insufficient to distinguish links from surrounding text for users with color vision deficiency. An underline provides a second, non-color visual indicator. WCAG 1.4.1 requires that color is not the only means of conveying information.

guidelines.guidance: Do not use this token for button text or standalone navigation items. Use color-action-primary for buttons and color-text-primary for navigation labels.
guidelines.rationale: Link color signals 'this text navigates elsewhere.' Applying it to buttons or navigation items conflates interactive patterns and creates confusion about the expected behavior.

guidelines.guidance: Do not modify the link color for visited states unless the context benefits from distinguishing visited and unvisited links (e.g., search results, documentation indexes).
guidelines.rationale: Most application interfaces do not benefit from visited link styling. It adds visual complexity without meaningful utility in task-oriented workflows.

guidelines.guidance: This color meets a 3.1:1 contrast ratio against color-text-primary (#1a1a1a) and a 7.9:1 contrast ratio against color-background-default (#ffffff).
guidelines.rationale: WCAG 1.4.1 requires a 3:1 contrast ratio between link text and surrounding body text when underline is not present. With an underline (as recommended), the 3:1 ratio against body text is a bonus, not a requirement. The 7.9:1 ratio against the background exceeds AA for all text sizes.

colorContrast.context: Link text on default page background in light mode.
Link text on subtle background (e.g., card surface) in light mode.

tokens.name: color-text-error
tokens.description: The color for error messages, inline validation text, and error state labels.

guidelines.guidance: Use for inline validation messages below form fields, error alert body text, and form field labels in an error state.
guidelines.rationale: Error text must be visually distinct from standard body text to draw the user's attention to the problem. A dedicated error color achieves this without requiring changes to font weight or size.

guidelines.guidance: Always pair error text with an error icon or a non-color indicator (e.g., a border color change on the input field).
guidelines.rationale: Approximately 8% of men and 0.5% of women have some form of color vision deficiency. Red text alone is not perceivable by all users. A second indicator ensures the error state is communicated through multiple channels.

examples.description: Error communicated through icon, border color, and text color — three independent channels.

presentation.code: [⚠ icon] [red border on input] 'Email address is required.'

examples.description: Only one channel (color) communicates the error. Fails for color-blind users.

presentation.code: 'Email address is required.' (red text only, no icon, no border change)

guidelines.guidance: Do not use for warnings, informational messages, or any non-error state. Use color-text-warning or color-text-primary for those contexts.
guidelines.rationale: Overusing the error color diminishes its signal value. If red text appears for non-critical messages, users learn to ignore it — including when it signals a real error.

guidelines.guidance: This color meets a 7.3:1 contrast ratio against color-background-default in light mode.
guidelines.rationale: Error text appears at body text sizes (14–16px), where a 4.5:1 ratio is the AA minimum. The 7.3:1 ratio exceeds AAA requirements.

guidelines.guidance: Do not rely on this color as the sole indicator of an error state. Combine with an icon, a border change, or an aria-describedby reference to the error message.
guidelines.rationale: WCAG 1.4.1 requires that information conveyed by color is also available through other means. WCAG 3.3.1 requires that input errors are identified and described to the user in text.

colorContrast.context: Error text on default page background in light mode.
Error text on a subtle error background (e.g., error alert container) in light mode.

tokens.name: color-text-success
tokens.description: The color for success messages and positive validation feedback.

guidelines.guidance: Use for success confirmation messages (e.g., 'Changes saved'), inline validation positive feedback, and success alert body text.
guidelines.rationale: Success text provides visual confirmation that an action completed as expected. A dedicated color distinguishes it from neutral and error states.

guidelines.guidance: Do not use for general informational messages or neutral status indicators.
guidelines.rationale: Overusing the success color trains users to overlook it. Reserve it for moments where an explicit positive outcome needs reinforcement.

guidelines.guidance: Always pair success text with an icon or other non-color indicator.
guidelines.rationale: Green is difficult to distinguish from red for users with protanopia or deuteranopia. A checkmark icon or other non-color signal ensures the success state is perceivable by all users.

guidelines.guidance: This color meets a 5.9:1 contrast ratio against color-background-default in light mode.
guidelines.rationale: Passes WCAG AA for all text sizes. Green tones are perceptually darker than red, so the system uses a darker green (700) to maintain adequate contrast.

colorContrast.context: Success text on default page background in light mode.

tokens.name: color-text-disabled
tokens.description: The text color for labels and content within disabled UI controls.

guidelines.guidance: Use only for text inside controls that are in a disabled state (buttons, inputs, checkboxes, select menus).
guidelines.rationale: Disabled text is intentionally low-contrast to communicate unavailability. Applying it outside disabled contexts creates confusion about whether content is interactive.

guidelines.guidance: Never use as a design choice for non-disabled content that should appear subdued. Use color-text-tertiary instead.
guidelines.rationale: Disabled styling has a specific semantic meaning. Using it for aesthetic dimming conflates visual presentation with interactive state, creating ambiguity for all users.

guidelines.guidance: Always pair with a disabled state on the parent control (e.g., disabled attribute, aria-disabled, reduced opacity).
guidelines.rationale: Text color alone is insufficient to communicate a disabled state. The parent control must also signal non-interactivity through cursor changes, pointer-event removal, and ARIA attributes.

guidelines.guidance: This color intentionally does not meet WCAG contrast minimums (1.7:1 against color-background-default). This is acceptable per WCAG 1.4.3, which exempts inactive UI components from contrast requirements.
guidelines.rationale: Disabled controls are exempt from minimum contrast requirements because they are not expected to be interacted with. The low contrast reinforces the non-interactive state visually.

colorContrast.context: Disabled text on default background. Intentionally below AA threshold — exempt per WCAG 1.4.3 for inactive components.

whenToUse.description: When applying color to text content rendered on standard light or dark background surfaces provided by the design system.
When building components that display body text, headings, labels, captions, or any typographic element that must adapt across color modes.
When communicating semantic meaning through text color, such as error messages, success confirmations, or interactive link text.

whenNotToUse.description: When placing text on a brand-colored, gradient, or image background where the system's pre-validated contrast pairings do not apply.

alternative.name: color-palette
alternative.rationale: Raw palette tokens allow manual contrast verification against non-standard backgrounds. Semantic text tokens are only validated against the system's default surface tokens.

whenNotToUse.description: When styling non-text elements such as icons, borders, or backgrounds that happen to need a similar hue.

alternative.name: color-icon
alternative.rationale: Icon tokens are tuned for the visual weight and contrast requirements of iconography, which differ from text. Using text tokens for icons couples unrelated concerns and causes unexpected results when either token set is updated.

whenNotToUse.description: When the text appears inside a component that supplies its own scoped color tokens (e.g., text on a filled button or a badge).

alternative.name: color-text-on-action
alternative.rationale: Component-scoped tokens account for the specific background they sit on. Applying a general text token to a scoped context risks contrast failures, especially across color modes.

guidelines.guidance: Use at most three levels of text emphasis on a single surface: primary, secondary, and tertiary.
guidelines.rationale: More than three levels of text emphasis are indistinguishable at a glance. Three levels provide enough hierarchy for heading, body, and supporting content without overloading the reader.

guidelines.guidance: Every surface must have at least one element using color-text-primary. Do not create surfaces where all text is secondary or tertiary.
guidelines.rationale: A surface without primary text lacks a clear entry point. The reader has no anchor to orient themselves. Primary text signals where to start reading.

guidelines.guidance: Do not mix text tokens from different semantic families (e.g., using color-text-error for non-error emphasis or color-text-link for non-link content).
guidelines.rationale: Semantic color tokens carry meaning. Using error red for emphasis or link blue for decoration erodes the user's ability to interpret color as a signal. When color loses meaning, the system loses a communication channel.

guidelines.guidance: When creating custom components, reference these semantic tokens rather than raw palette tokens (e.g., use color-text-primary instead of color.neutral.900).
guidelines.rationale: Semantic tokens adapt across color modes (light, dark, high contrast). Raw palette tokens do not. Using semantic tokens ensures custom components work correctly across all modes without per-mode overrides.

guidelines.guidance: All text tokens in this group (except color-text-disabled) meet WCAG 2.1 AA contrast requirements against their intended background surfaces.
guidelines.rationale: AA compliance is the minimum standard for production interfaces. Tokens are pre-validated at the system level so that individual component authors do not need to manually verify contrast for standard combinations.

guidelines.guidance: Do not modify the resolved values of these tokens at the component or application level. Override only by switching color modes (light, dark, high contrast) via the system's theming mechanism.
guidelines.rationale: Direct value overrides bypass the system's contrast validation. A hardcoded color may pass contrast in one mode but fail in another. The theming mechanism ensures all mode-specific values have been verified.
